summary: Nadia announces she's leaving GitHub to join Protocol Labs as a researcher focused on developing new economic models for software production. She reflects on the broader challenge of understanding incentives in digital infrastructure, noting that current research doesn't adequately address modern challenges. She'll also be a visiting Fellow at Harvard Kennedy School. The post frames open source software as a valuable lens for understanding behavioral patterns because of its thorough documentation.
key_points: Current research doesn't adequately explain how to structure incentives for digital infrastructure, platforms, and online content
Open source software's thorough documentation makes it ideal for studying behavioral patterns around production
Paid research opportunities exploring these questions are extremely rare in tech
Protocol Labs represents a rare organization willing to tackle hard problems with long timelines
money_quotes: We know shockingly little about how to structure incentives in our modern world, and current research doesn't have much to say about today's challenges.
Open source software, because it's so thoroughly documented, is a great way to understand these behavioral patterns.
It turns out that thinking about these problems on my nights and weekends is a recipe for creative exhaustion.
There are few companies who not only share my interest in solving hard problems with long timelines, but who structure their organization and funding model accordingly.
key_insight: Open source software serves as an ideal laboratory for understanding the broader challenge of structuring incentives for digital goods production, but finding institutional support for this research is exceptionally rare.
